guess my question is, we have a lot to cover in our platform.

Let me...

Pop something up and I'll just try and do this in like two minutes, but I'll sort of give you like a macro view of like how we think about what we do and then align that quickly to like pillars of our platform and product in two slides.
And so when we think about like this world of conversational AI and CX based AI, there's a ton of focus right now in the market on just like automate everything with AI agents.
We do that, right?

We have a market leading AI agent product, but philosophically, we think there's like a much bigger opportunity and that like a true strategy around maximizing the value of customer conversations and driving a better customer experience.
taking that from the point of view of like sort of automation in a vacuum is the wrong strategy.
And we think of this like sort of universe of possibility around conversations as bucketing conversations into four categories.
And so when you think about those categories, they're like conversations that you have with businesses that just, they shouldn't happen in the first place, right?
Like they're not valuable to the customer.
They're not valuable to the business.
They're sort of driven by like more macro issues outside of the contact center or customer care operation, right?
They're related to like issues in the digital experience, product problems, policies, bad communications, et cetera.
Those shouldn't be automated with AI agents.
They should be solved from a root cause point of view.

But in order to do that and in order to sort of build the case to do that across like the various business units and teams that touch those types of things, need to understand and be able to quantify what they are, understand the root cause, what the solution would be, and like build a case that it's worthwhile to do that actually is grounded on ROI.
And so you should solve that problem with cost and CX saving insights, deep contextual insight into conversations.
Number two, there's...
There's a ton of conversations out there where AI agents and automation can add a lot of value, right?
Like these are scenarios in which we just want to solve a problem or get some information or get something done.
And like the value to the end customer is efficiency.
Like it picks up on the first ring.
It solves my problem as efficiently and as effectively as possible.
It doesn't get it wrong.
All of those types of things should be automated via AI agents.
And they're not just simple things, right?
They can be complex things.
But the point is that they are relatively transactional in nature where like I'm trying to solve a problem.
And like there's a straightforward outcome to that thing.
The reason I make that distinction is we also believe that there's a subset of conversations that shouldn't be automated at all.
And those are really high emotional value conversations or highly emotionally leveraged from a customer point of view or extremely high value to the business.
And so that's like, you know, I've had a disaster.
And I'm calling my insurance company to figure out options.
I probably don't want to talk to an AI agent that's calibrated to the average scenario.
I want some human empathy and somebody that's thinking about me and my family and my situation and those types of things.
Or I'm a VIP customer with massive lifetime months, the average AI agent dealing with that scenario and further  that individual off.
So there is a subset of conversations where even if AI agents are capable of handling them, that it's not in the best interest of the customer or the business to actually automate those away.
we should augment humans and give them the tools to be as effective as possible in dealing with those conversations and as productive as possible to drive down the cost to serve.
And then finally, there's like a subset of conversations that aren't happening at all, right?
Like due to constraints and costs and human capabilities, like businesses know that there are things that they could be and should be doing as it relates to proactive.
Outreach and value add and ways they can engage their customers beyond just waiting for.
to come to them with a problem.
But like throwing humans at that is too expensive.
And like the simplest example there is like for our service.
But there's much more complex examples related to like proactive engagement and outreach.

So this sort of idea of where the opportunities lie across the universe of conversations and how you should deal with them informs our overall product strategy, which we sort of align to these three pillars.
We want to help businesses deeply understand what's happening within their conversations and why, down to root cause analysis.
We do that with Cresta Insights and AI analysts, which Stu will show you.

We want to augment every element of human work that's happening, whether that's the frontline agents and giving them behavioral guidance in real time, serving them up with knowledge assistance, automating workflows and typing and summarization for them, as well as the management layer.
And so giving people who evaluate conversations and coaching agents tools they need to automate some of that work, be more precise, save a lot of time and be more prescriptive.

And then finally, automate the conversations that it makes sense to automate and do so really effectively and use all of those insights that we talked about to understand what we should automate, how to automate it, optimize those AI agents.

And when they do need to escalate it, because they will, we want to, again, pass them off in a way that's seamless and contextually driven to human agents that are augmented with all of the AI that I talked about.
So that's like the 10,000 foot view of our product suite and sort of why our product suite is the way it is.
And then Stu can bring it to life in a demo.
But any questions based on that overview?

Yeah, I guess just on this slide here, is this how you guys see kind of the maturity of like new customers that may be kind of engaging Crest for the first time or maybe looking to implement AI for the first time in their, you know, CX operations of like getting the analyze put together first, then move to augment, then automate, or is there kind of a piecemeal way to go about?
It's completely different.
We're modular, and we want to meet customers where they are.

We mostly serve very large Fortune 500-type customers, and so what they're currently funded to do, what they already have in place in some of these areas, there's overlap across various software categories, and they may be in different states of maturity across these pillars.

So we don't try to overprescribe, I think with a totally blank slate, doing it this way would make a lot of sense and de-risk things for the business, to some extent, is moving from left to right here.

And so if customers are coming to us asking for more of a consultative strategy, saying, we don't have any of this in place, there's no incumbent software, we want to basically drive a transformation initiative, then we would talk about putting insights in place, augmenting humans, automating away 68% of the quality management process, enabling those people to coach more effectively, and then building AI agents based on all of those insights and learning.
But it can look totally different.
We started with CVS Health just summarizing 100% of their conversations.
Now they're running predictive insight, tickle time, augmentation of their humans.

Another interesting example there that's sort of a little bit more around perception and policy is they discovered through analysis that a lot of customers didn't like the fact that they only get access to the upgrade lists, the sort of like wait list to get an upgrade three days before their flight, and they were able to build a case using all of this data, showing perception, digging into the transcripts and the evidence to expand the time frame for people to be able to access those lists up to 30 days in advance for the flight, which was a big win from a CSAT perspective for an important segment of customers.
